title: Octovian (Cambridge, University Library, MS Ff.2.38)
summary: The repository artifact is a pinned copy of University of Michigan CME item OctC, which encodes Frances McSparran’s direct working transcription of the Northern Octavian in Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38, ff. 90rb-102vb. The source statement explicitly calls the text a transcription rather than the 1986 EETS edition: manuscript spelling, capitalization, and punctuation are retained, abbreviations are silently expanded, and even EETS page milestones are added only for comparison. The 1,731-line witness-specific text is distinct from the Lincoln Northern text and from the Southern Octavian. OTA item 1683 is a closely related three-file release, but its protected bitstreams were not accepted or compared, so exact package identity remains supported rather than confirmed. Complete public source objects for DIMEV v1, the 1857 CUL catalog volume, and Harriet Hudson’s 2006 edition are cached outside Git. No verified public whole-manuscript digital facsimile of Ff.2.38 was found; onsite, mediated viewing, and paid imaging routes are recorded separately from the 1979 Scolar printed facsimile.
review_notes: The cataloging boundary is the complete 1,731-line Cambridge witness text at ff. 90rb-102vb, not an abstract Northern version, the Lincoln witness, the Southern version, the 1986 EETS volume, the OTA package, or the physical codex as a whole.

The manuscript’s mid-fifteenth-century date and Leicestershire linguistic profile describe the witness and scribal language. They are not substituted for the work-level composition range or qualified Northeast Midlands/Yorkshire localization.

The 1857 catalog’s statement of a uniform hand conflicts with the current Database of Middle English Romance description of two scribes, the second taking over during Octavian; both claims are preserved and the exact hand boundary remains unresolved.

Rights and access statements are component-specific. The medieval text, McSparran transcription, U-M encoding, OTA package, repository copy, modern editions, printed facsimile, and any CUL-supplied photography are separate layers.
Negative public-facsimile findings are date-bounded to 2026-07-15 and do not establish that no institutional, private, legacy, or unindexed surrogate exists.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for OctC
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository’s conversion workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The 160,248-byte repository file was byte-identical to the same path on the current CME main branch at review time.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item OctC
entities.description: The U-M structural encoding of McSparran’s Cambridge transcription, distributed in 1993 and now represented by the current CME collection and the pinned repository XML.
notes: The exact current item resolver was intermittently protected by an automated challenge; the collection-level current page and pinned item XML provide the evidence used here.

entities.label: Oxford Text Archive item 1683, Octovian
entities.description: A current OTA item listing for a three-file electronic release containing a header plus separate Cambridge (OctC) and Lincoln (OctL) texts.
notes: The current listing names header1683.xml, octc-1683.txt, and octl-1683.txt. The content bitstreams require an interactive terms step and were not accepted or cached during this audit.

The current OTA preview describes the Cambridge text through the EETS edition, whereas the repository XML gives the more precise provenance statement that OctC derives from a direct McSparran transcription and merely imports EETS pagination.

entities.label: Frances McSparran’s direct transcription of the Cambridge Northern Octavian
entities.description: The direct working transcription of Ff.2.38 used to create the electronic text. This entity is typed as a scholarly edition only because the schema has no distinct transcription type; the source explicitly says it is not an edition.
notes: No separately cataloged physical or digital carrier for the working transcription was identified.
Its exact relationship to McSparran’s editorial files for the 1986 EETS volume remains unresolved.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38
entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century paper romance and didactic miscellany preserving the 1,731-line Cambridge Northern Octavian at ff. 90rb-102vb.

holding.notes: The principal public item-level description remains the 1857 printed catalog; most CUL medieval manuscripts do not yet have modern ArchiveSearch records.

physical_description.format: folio codex, chiefly double columns of about forty lines
notes: The 1857 catalog reports missing leaves and calls the hand uniform.
The current Database of Middle English Romance reports two scribes, with the second taking over in the middle of Octavian.
DIMEV assigns the Octavian hand LALME/eLALME LP 531, grid 445 305, Leicestershire; this is a scribal-language localization, not a secure place of manuscript production or work composition.
Octavian begins after Sir Tryamour on f. 90r and Bevis of Hampton begins on f. 102v; those shared page boundaries do not make the adjacent works part of OctC.
DIMEV’s exact target locus ff. 90rb-102vb is preferred to the less precise ff. 90r-102v summaries.

entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral Library MS 91
entities.description: The other complete manuscript witness of the Northern Octavian, at DIMEV’s ff. 98va-109rb. DIMEV v1 counts 1,628 lines and records lacunae, while the current Database of Middle English Romance manuscript page gives 1,629 lines; the disagreement is not reconciled. It is related to but not the textual source of OctC.
notes: McSparran prints its transcription on the odd EETS pages 77-179; Hudson uses it as the base manuscript for the 2006 METS text.

DIMEV v1 reports 1,628 manuscript-text lines; the current DMR manuscript page reports 1,629. Separately, the OctL electronic component contains 1,634 unique consecutive L/@N positions. That encoding metric is not used to harmonize the one-line catalog disagreement.

entities.label: Harriet Hudson’s Four Middle English Romances, second edition

bibliographic.citation: Harriet Hudson, ed., Four Middle English Romances, 2nd ed. (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2006).
bibliographic.title: Four Middle English Romances
bibliographic.pages: 206 PDF pages; Octavian introduction pp. 39-43, text pp. 45-88, notes pp. 89-95

notes: Hudson edits the Lincoln text, collating and occasionally supplying from Cambridge; the edition is not the source text encoded as OctC.

The prose introduction correctly identifies Cambridge as Northern, but the manuscript list mistakenly labels it Southern and gives ff. 90r-101v; those isolated errors are preserved as an inconsistency rather than adopted.

rights.component: anonymous medieval Northern Octavian text and witness readings
rights.basis: The work and manuscript witness are medieval and predate modern copyright by centuries.
notes: This does not determine rights in modern transcriptions, editions, photography, encodings, interfaces, or supplied files.

rights.component: U-M CME encoded text and collection presentation
rights.basis: The current collection page describes manually keyed full texts from public-domain materials and applies No Copyright - United States at collection level.
notes: The current exact item resolver could not be independently inspected through the automated challenge, so the assertion is not promoted beyond collection-level evidence.

rights.component: U-M-derived encoded text embedded in the pinned XML
rights.basis: The XML embeds a legacy OTA statement limiting the text to personal scholarly use, while current U-M collection metadata applies NoC-US and current OTA item metadata applies CC BY 3.0. The conflict cannot be resolved by silently choosing the most permissive statement.
notes: Confirm the provenance and supersession of the legacy restriction before redistribution in a commercial or print-on-demand product.

rights.component: contributor-controlled CME repository software and material covered by the root LICENSE
rights.basis: The CME repository root LICENSE uses the Unlicense, but its scope over inherited U-M and OTA textual layers is not explicit and is not treated as superseding their separate statements.

rights.component: current OTA item 1683 package as labeled by the item page
rights.basis: The current OTA item metadata applies Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 to the item. The content files were not accepted or inspected for additional embedded terms.
notes: Do not generalize this current package-level label to the repository XML without resolving version and supersession questions.

rights.component: McSparran’s direct working transcription and editorial choices
rights.basis: Authorship is documented, but no independently verified license or publication status for the working transcription carrier was found.

rights.component: 1986 introduction, parallel transcriptions, apparatus, pagination, layout, and editorial matter
rights.basis: The modern scholarly edition was published in 1986 by Oxford University Press for EETS; no open license was identified.

rights.component: any CUL-supplied photography, scan, or other reproduction
rights.basis: CUL limits supplied copies to research, private study, or evaluation absent a separate license and requires prior written permission for reproduction and publication.
notes: Payment for image supply alone does not authorize public Git hosting, redistribution, publication, or print-on-demand reuse.

rights.component: 1979 facsimile photography, introduction, selection, layout, and print edition
rights.basis: The modern Scolar Press facsimile was published in 1979 and no open license was identified.

rights.component: Hudson’s editorial text, introductions, notes, formatted digital edition, and complete downloadable PDF
rights.basis: METS states that its digital editions are for individual use and directs reprint requests to the Executive Director. Its separate CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 grant is specific to XML and is not generalized to the PDF.

rights.component: METS XML supplied separately from the formatted edition and PDF
rights.basis: METS licenses its XML under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International.

rights.component: DIMEV version 1.0 data, documentation, and release archive
rights.basis: The DIMEV version 1.0 release includes a CC BY 4.0 license.
The catalog was published in 1857, far before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works.

editorial_practices.description: McSparran silently expands manuscript abbreviations, so the encoded letter sequence is not an allographic diplomatic transcript.

editorial_practices.representation: retained rather than normalized, apart from silent abbreviation expansion
editorial_practices.description: The source statement says the text otherwise preserves manuscript spelling, capitalization, and punctuation.
notes: The controlled practice name is used to record an explicit non-normalization policy.

editorial_practices.source_feature: working transcription and EETS editorial package
editorial_practices.representation: continuous Cambridge verse text with front matter, back matter, and editorial notes omitted
editorial_practices.description: The electronic text includes the witness transcription but omits printed-edition front and back matter and editorial notes.

editorial_practices.source_feature: continuous 1,731-line transcription without encoded stanza groups
editorial_practices.representation: 18 artificial DIV1 blocks, normally 100 lines each, with supplied headings
editorial_practices.description: The encoding adds navigational hundred-line groups. They are representation units, not manuscript, narrative, or stanza divisions.

editorial_practices.representation: 52 PB milestones labeled with every even page from 76 through 178
editorial_practices.description: U-M supplies EETS page markers solely to facilitate comparison with the final edition; the page sequence is not manuscript foliation.

editorial_practices.source_feature: correspondence between Cambridge and Lincoln textual lines
editorial_practices.representation: one XPTR per OctC line; 331 TO values are 0000, while 1,400 nonzero occurrences resolve to 1,397 unique values in an undocumented target namespace
editorial_practices.description: The XPTR/@TO values exhibit partial cross-version alignment or concordance behavior rather than OctC line numbering: OctC and the paired OctL component each have 1,400 nonzero occurrences and 1,397 unique TO values, but their unique-value intersection is only 1,180, with 217 values unique to each file. Because no explicit field documentation or target-namespace definition was located, the exact semantics remain unresolved and the values are not used to calculate textual extent.
editorial_practices.scope: 1,731 L/XPTR pairs in OctC and comparative OctL pointer audit
notes: The independent L/@N sequence 0001-1731 and specialist catalogs establish the 1,731-line extent.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Lincoln manuscript text without indicated stanza divisions, tag lines, or modern punctuation
editorial_practices.representation: editorially supplied punctuation and stanza spacing
editorial_practices.description: Hudson supplies punctuation and formats stanzas for readers; those display units must not be projected backward onto the Cambridge manuscript or OctC encoding.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Lincoln base text with lacunae and variant Cambridge redaction
editorial_practices.representation: Lincoln-based edited text with Cambridge comparison and selected supplies
editorial_practices.description: Hudson treats Lincoln as base while consulting the shorter, independently transmitted Cambridge text; this does not make Hudson a transcription of Cambridge.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/OctC.xml, at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for identity, McSparran responsibility, direct-transcription provenance, 1993 U-M distribution, legacy OTA restriction and identifier, 1350-1375 profile date, transcription policy, omissions, EETS comparison pagination, and complete encoded readings.

evidence.citation: XPath, hash, Git-object, and OctC/OctL pointer inspection of the electronic Octavian texts.
evidence.locator: DIV1, HEAD, L/@N, PB/@N, EPB, LG, NOTE, P, and XPTR/@TO counts and distributions
evidence.summary: OctC has 18 supplied DIV1 groups, 19 HEAD, 1,731 unique consecutive L/@N values 0001-1731, 52 PB values covering even EETS pp. 76-178, 53 EPB, no LG or NOTE, five header P elements, and 1,731 XPTR. OctC has 331 TO=0000 values and 1,400 nonzero occurrences resolving to 1,397 unique values. The paired OctL component also has 1,400 nonzero occurrences and 1,397 unique values; the unique-value intersection is 1,180, with 217 values unique to each. This shows partial cross-version alignment or concordance behavior, but the target namespace is undocumented and TO values are not used as textual extent. The file is 160,248 bytes and Git blob 23ab964fbecf7a0ea9b92feae1d3a319dbb8c816.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME paired Lincoln XML, source/CME_phase_1-2/OctL.xml, at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c; structural inspection on 2026-07-15.
evidence.locator: L/@N and XPTR/@TO counts and distributions
evidence.summary: The 153,520-byte paired OctL file, Git blob 37c33ddd84ab03845bd0b334fcb3e256a5316ffe, contains 1,634 unique consecutive L/@N positions 0001-1634. Its 1,634 XPTR elements comprise 234 TO=0000 values and 1,400 nonzero occurrences resolving to 1,397 unique values. The 1,634 encoded positions are a separate digital representation metric and do not resolve DIMEV’s 1,628-line versus DMR’s 1,629-line manuscript-text counts.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME git repository, pinned and current-main OctC objects.
evidence.summary: The submodule commit and current remote main resolved to the same commit during review, and the raw OctC file was byte-identical.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME root LICENSE (The Unlicense).
evidence.summary: The repository root applies the Unlicense to contributor-controlled material, but does not explicitly resolve scope over inherited U-M and OTA source-text layers.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Octavian.
evidence.summary: Current specialist synthesis for c. 1350 composition, Northeast Midlands and possible Yorkshire localization, Northern and Southern forms, plot, subjects, Cambridge and Lincoln witnesses, print fragment, and modern editions.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Cambridge, University Library, MS Ff.2.38.
evidence.locator: manuscript description and contents, especially Octavian at ff. 90r-102v
evidence.summary: Supports the mid-fifteenth-century paper witness, dimensions, two scribes with the second taking over during Octavian, complete manuscript context, shared work boundaries, Northern identity, 1,731 lines, and 1979 Scolar facsimile.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Lincoln, Cathedral Library, MS 91 (Lincoln Thornton Manuscript).

evidence.summary: The current manuscript page identifies the Northern Octavian at ff. 98v-109r and reports 1,629 lines. This differs by one line from DIMEV v1’s 1,628-line count and is preserved as an unresolved catalog disagreement.

evidence.citation: Harriet Hudson, ed., Four Middle English Romances, 2nd ed. (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2006).
evidence.locator: general introduction pp. 1-3; Octavian introduction pp. 39-43; text pp. 45-88; notes pp. 89-95
evidence.summary: Supports anonymous authorship, composition and localization, family-romance and tail-rhyme classification, Old French adaptation, Northern/Southern distinction, written Cambridge-Lincoln relation, Lincoln-base editorial method, Cambridge supplies, and supplied stanza/punctuation practice. Its isolated Cambridge manuscript-list errors are not adopted.

open_questions.question: How exactly do the McSparran working transcription, OTA 1683 files, 1993 U-M OctC encoding, current OTA CC BY 3.0 label, current U-M NoC-US statement, embedded personal-scholarly-use restriction, and CME Unlicense relate and supersede one another?
next_steps: Ask OTA and U-M for accession, migration, license, and file-history documentation for 1683 / U-1683-A and OctC.
With authorization, accept the OTA click-through in a normal browser, download the complete three-file package, and compare octc-1683.txt and header1683.xml with the repository XML.
Obtain explicit permission or a provider clarification before commercial or print-on-demand redistribution.

open_questions.question: Can CUL supply or publish a complete high-resolution color surrogate of Ff.2.38, and what exact reuse terms and cost would govern it?
next_steps: Ask Manuscripts staff through the Virtual Reading Room whether complete existing photography is held.
Request a quote for the complete bound object and a separate target-only quote for ff. 90rb-102vb.
Request written redistribution, publication, and commercial-use terms before acquisition.

open_questions.question: At which exact line or folio does the second scribe take over during Octavian, and how should that hand boundary qualify LP 531 and any line-level language claims?
next_steps: Inspect the 1979 facsimile introduction and target leaves or request a CUL visual review.
Consult current codicological scholarship and eLALME’s hand-level description before assigning the profile to all 1,731 lines.

open_questions.question: Does a separately preserved McSparran transcription carrier survive, and what is its precise textual relationship to the 1986 EETS Cambridge transcription?
next_steps: Ask U-M, OTA, EETS/OUP, and relevant McSparran archival custodians for production files and accession notes.
Compare the repository text line by line with the EETS even-page transcription when lawful access is available.

open_questions.question: What exact line-by-line crosswalk links OctC, the EETS Cambridge and Lincoln transcriptions, and the XPTR concordance target universe?
next_steps: Locate explicit HTI/DLPS or project documentation for XPTR TARGORDER, FROM, and TO in this paired edition.
Compare both complete electronic components against the EETS parallel pages, preserving unmatched and many-to-one mappings.
